problem_spec.summary: Count the distinct roots on the unit circle of a polynomial designed so that, after the substitution z=e^{iθ}, both terms share the same complex phase and the equation collapses to a real trigonometric identity solvable via the triple-angle formula.
givens: Restrict attention to roots z with |z|=1.
ask: Determine the number of distinct complex roots of P(z) on the unit circle.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Defines the family P_n(z)=(z+1)^{2n}-(z^n+1)^2; the instance uses n=3 (so degree 6 vs squared degree 3).

invariant(s).name: unit_circle_phase_alignment
invariant(s).statement: For z=e^{iθ}, (z+1)^{2n} and (z^n+1)^2 both carry the phase factor e^{inθ}, so P_n(e^{iθ})=0 reduces to a purely real equation 2^{2n-2} cos^{2n}(θ/2) = cos^2(nθ/2).

invariant(s).name: triple_angle_closure_for_n3
invariant(s).statement: When n=3, the reduced equation involves cos(3x), allowing elimination via cos(3x)=4cos^3 x - 3cos x to obtain a polynomial equation in cos x.

essential_assumption(s): z lies on the unit circle so z=e^{iθ} with θ real.
Cancelling the common factor e^{i3θ} is valid since it is never zero.
